headline: RM82 billion in local currency trade sounds like de-dollarization — 76% of it is with one country
context: Cumulative local currency settlements with China, Thailand, and Indonesia reached RM82.1B over 16 years. China accounts for 76%. Against RM2.9 trillion in annual trade, it's early-stage. The hedging infrastructure to manage the new exposures barely exists.

cards.big: RM82.1B in local currency trade over 16 years — real growth, but 76% is with China and the hedging infrastructure barely exists
cards.sub: Local currency settlement with China reached 25.6% of bilateral trade, up from 1.2% in 2009. Thailand 17.2%, Indonesia 15.8%. 74.4% of Malaysia-China trade is still in USD.

cards.big: The 16-year cumulative equals roughly one month of annual trade — but within the China corridor, a quarter now bypasses the dollar
cards.sub: SMEs save up to 2% in fees. Palm oil, LNG and petroleum stay USD-benchmarked. B40 feels ringgit weakness through imported inflation.

cards.big: 76% concentration in China isn't diversification — and BRICS partner status carries tariff risk without decision-making power
cards.sub: BNM-PBOC swap at CNY180B/RM110B dwarfs all others. 68% of E&E exports to the US are semiconductors, ~65% from US firms. The geopolitical risk is asymmetric.

cards.big: The 1998 capital controls that saved the ringgit now block its internationalisation — incentives to keep them run deeper than economics
cards.sub: No deep MYR derivatives. Retail links (DuitNow-PromptPay-QRIS) outpace wholesale. BNM ruled out new controls but has no roadmap to relax old ones.

cards.big: The question isn't whether to reduce dollar dependency — it's whether Malaysia is building yuan dependency instead, and who bears the cost
Publish annual volumes by country, build the hedging infrastructure exporters need, and stop conflating transaction savings with structural independence
